Since the 15th century, the **administrative divisions of China** have consisted of several levels due to [China](/wiki/China "China")'s large [population](/wiki/Demographics_of_China "Demographics of China") and [geographical area](/wiki/Geography_of_China "Geography of China"). The [Ministry of Civil Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Civil_Affairs "Ministry of Civil Affairs") determines that there are four levels of divisions: provincial-level, prefecture-level, county-level, and township-level.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Since the 17th century, provincial-level division boundaries in China have remained largely static. After the [establishment of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Establishment_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Establishment of the People's Republic of China") in 1949, major changes include the reorganization of provinces in the [Northeast](/wiki/Northeast_China "Northeast China") and the formation of [autonomous regions](/wiki/Autonomous_regions_of_China "Autonomous regions of China"), based on the [subdivisions of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_the_Soviet_Union "Subdivisions of the Soviet Union"). Provincial-level divisions serve an important cultural role in China, as people tend to identify with their native province.

## Levels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_divisions_of_China&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Levels")]

The [Ministry of Civil Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Civil_Affairs "Ministry of Civil Affairs") determines that there are four levels of divisions: provincial-level, prefecture-level, county-level, and township-level.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Each of the levels, except for the provincial-level special administrative regions, corresponds to a level in the [civil service](/wiki/Civil_Service_of_China "Civil Service of China").

### Provincial level (1st)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_divisions_of_China&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Provincial level (1st)")]

Main article: [Province-level divisions of China](/wiki/Province-level_divisions_of_China "Province-level divisions of China")

The [People's Republic of China](/wiki/China "China") (PRC) lays claims to 34 [province-level divisions](/wiki/Province-level_divisions_of_China "Province-level divisions of China"), including 22 provinces, 5 autonomous regions, 4 municipalities, 2 special administrative regions, and 1 claimed province.

Provinces are theoretically subordinate to the PRC central government, but in practice, provincial officials have considerable discretion over economic policy. Unlike in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), the central government's power was (with the exception of the military) not exercised through a parallel set of institutions until the early 1990s. The actual practical power of the provinces has created what some economists call "[federalism with Chinese characteristics](/wiki/Chinese_federalism "Chinese federalism")".

Most of the [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_China "Provinces of China"), with the exception of those in the [northeast](/wiki/Northeast_China "Northeast China"), have boundaries which were established long ago in the [Yuan](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty"), [Ming](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty"), and [Qing](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") dynasties. Sometimes provincial borders coincide with cultural or geographic boundaries. This was an attempt by the imperial government to discourage [separatism](/wiki/Separatism "Separatism") and warlordism through a [divide and rule](/wiki/Divide_and_rule "Divide and rule") policy. Nevertheless, provinces have come to serve an important cultural role in China. People tend to be identified in terms of their native provinces, and each province has a stereotype associated with its inhabitants.

The most recent administrative changes have included the elevation of [Hainan](/wiki/Hainan "Hainan") (1988) and [Chongqing](/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing") (1997) to provincial level status, and the designation of [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") (1997) and [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau") (1999) as [special administrative regions](/wiki/Special_administrative_regions_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Special administrative regions of the People's Republic of China").

Map of China's population density on prefecture level, based on the official 2022 census

Prefectural level divisions or second-level divisions are the second level of the administrative structure. Most provinces are divided into only prefecture-level cities and contain no other second level administrative units. Of the 22 provinces and 5 autonomous regions, only 3 provinces ([Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan"), [Guizhou](/wiki/Guizhou "Guizhou"), [Qinghai](/wiki/Qinghai "Qinghai")) and 1 autonomous region ([Xinjiang](/wiki/Xinjiang "Xinjiang")) have more than three second-level or prefectural-level divisions that are not prefecture-level cities. As of June 2020, there were 339 prefectural level divisions:

|  |
| --- |
| **7 [Prefectures](/wiki/Prefectures_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China#Prefecture "Prefectures of the People's Republic of China")**: formerly the dominant second level division, thus this administrative level is often called "prefectural level". They were mostly replaced by [prefecture-level cities](/wiki/Prefecture-level_city "Prefecture-level city") from 1983 to the 1990s. Today, prefectures exist only in [Heilongjiang](/wiki/Heilongjiang "Heilongjiang"), [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet_Autonomous_Region "Tibet Autonomous Region") and [Xinjiang](/wiki/Xinjiang "Xinjiang"). |
| **30 [Autonomous prefectures](/wiki/Autonomous_prefecture "Autonomous prefecture")**: prefectures with one or more designated [ethnic minorities](/wiki/List_of_Chinese_nationalities "List of Chinese nationalities"), mostly in China's western regions. |
| **299 [prefecture-level cities](/wiki/Prefecture-level_city "Prefecture-level city")**: the largest number of prefectural-level divisions, generally composed of an urban center and surrounding rural areas much larger than the urban core and thus not "[cities](/wiki/Cities "Cities")" but municipalities in the strict sense of the term |
| **3 [Leagues](/wiki/Leagues_of_China "Leagues of China")**: effectively the same as prefectures, but found only in [Inner Mongolia](/wiki/Inner_Mongolia "Inner Mongolia"). Like prefectures, leagues have mostly been replaced with prefecture-level cities. The unique name is a holdover from earlier forms of administration in Mongolia. |

### Basic level autonomy (5th)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_divisions_of_China&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Basic level autonomy (5th)")]

Main article: [Villages of China](/wiki/Villages_of_China "Villages of China")

The **basic level autonomy** serves as an organizational division (census, mail system) and does not have much importance in political representative power. Basic local divisions such as [neighborhoods](/wiki/Neighborhoods "Neighborhoods") and [communities](/wiki/Community_(China) "Community (China)") are not informal, but have defined boundaries and elected heads (one per area):

In urban areas, every subdistrict of a district of a city administers many [communities](/wiki/Residential_community "Residential community") or [residential committees](/wiki/Neighborhoods "Neighborhoods"). Each of them has a residential committee to administer the dwellers of that neighborhood or community. Rural areas are organized into [village committees](/wiki/Village_committee "Village committee") or villager groups. A "village" in this case can either be a natural village, one that spontaneously and naturally exists, or a virtual village, which is a bureaucratic entity.

### Special cases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_divisions_of_China&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Special cases")]

Five cities formally on prefectural level have a special status in regard to planning and budget. They are separately listed in the five-year and annual state plans on the same level as provinces and national ministries, making them economically independent of their provincial government. These [cities specifically designated in the state plan](/w/index.php?title=Cities_specifically_designated_in_the_state_plan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cities specifically designated in the state plan (page does not exist)") (Chinese: 计划单列市) are

* [Dalian](/wiki/Dalian "Dalian") ([Liaoning](/wiki/Liaoning "Liaoning"))
* [Ningbo](/wiki/Ningbo "Ningbo") ([Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang"))
* [Qingdao](/wiki/Qingdao "Qingdao") ([Shandong](/wiki/Shandong "Shandong"))
* [Shenzhen](/wiki/Shenzhen "Shenzhen") ([Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong"))
* [Xiamen](/wiki/Xiamen "Xiamen") ([Fujian](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian"))

In terms of budget authority, their governments have the *de facto* status of a province, but their legislative organs ([National People's Congress](/wiki/National_People%27s_Congress "National People's Congress") and [Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference](/wiki/Chinese_People%27s_Political_Consultative_Conference "Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference")) and other authorities not related to the economy are on the level of a prefecture and under the leadership of the province.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Some other large prefecture-level cities, known as [sub-provincial cities](/wiki/Sub-provincial_divisions_in_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Sub-provincial divisions in the People's Republic of China"), are *half a level* below a province. The [mayors](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") of these cities have the same [rank](/wiki/Political_position_ranking_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Political position ranking of the People's Republic of China") as a vice [governor](/wiki/Governor#People's_Republic_of_China "Governor") of a province, and their district governments are half a rank higher than those of normal districts. The capitals of some provinces (seat of provincial government) are sub-provincial cities. In addition to the five cities specifically designated in the state plan, sub-provincial cities are:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* [Harbin](/wiki/Harbin "Harbin") ([Heilongjiang](/wiki/Heilongjiang "Heilongjiang"))
* [Changchun](/wiki/Changchun "Changchun") ([Jilin](/wiki/Jilin "Jilin"))
* [Shenyang](/wiki/Shenyang "Shenyang") ([Liaoning](/wiki/Liaoning "Liaoning"))
* [Jinan](/wiki/Jinan "Jinan") ([Shandong](/wiki/Shandong "Shandong"))
* [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing") ([Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu"))
* [Hangzhou](/wiki/Hangzhou "Hangzhou") ([Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang"))
* [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") ([Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong"))
* [Wuhan](/wiki/Wuhan "Wuhan") ([Hubei](/wiki/Hubei "Hubei"))
* [Chengdu](/wiki/Chengdu "Chengdu") ([Sichuan](/wiki/Sichuan "Sichuan"))
* [Xi'an](/wiki/Xi%27an "Xi'an") ([Shaanxi](/wiki/Shaanxi "Shaanxi"))

A similar case exists with some county-level cities. Some county-level cities are given more autonomy. These cities are known as [sub-prefecture-level cities](/wiki/Sub-prefecture-level_cities "Sub-prefecture-level cities"), meaning that they are given a level of power higher than a county, but still lower than a prefecture. Such cities are also *half a level* higher than what they would normally be. Sub-prefecture-level cities are often not put into any prefecture (i.e. they are directly administered by their province). Examples of sub-prefecture-level cities include [Jiyuan](/wiki/Jiyuan "Jiyuan") ([Henan](/wiki/Henan "Henan") province), [Xiantao](/wiki/Xiantao "Xiantao"), [Qianjiang](/wiki/Qianjiang,_Hubei "Qianjiang, Hubei") and [Tianmen](/wiki/Tianmen "Tianmen") ([Hubei](/wiki/Hubei "Hubei")), [Golmud](/wiki/Golmud "Golmud") ([Qinghai](/wiki/Qinghai "Qinghai")), [Manzhouli](/wiki/Manzhouli "Manzhouli") ([Inner Mongolia](/wiki/Inner_Mongolia "Inner Mongolia")), [Shihanza](/wiki/Shihezi "Shihezi"), [Tumushuk](/wiki/Tumushuk "Tumushuk"), [Aral](/wiki/Aral,_Xinjiang "Aral, Xinjiang"), and [Wujiaqu](/wiki/Wujiaqu "Wujiaqu") ([Xinjiang](/wiki/Xinjiang "Xinjiang")).

Some districts are also placed at *half a level* higher that what it should be. Examples are [Pudong](/wiki/Pudong "Pudong"), [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") and [Binhai](/wiki/Binhai "Binhai"), [Tianjin](/wiki/Tianjin "Tianjin"). Although its status as a [district](/wiki/District_of_China "District of China") of a [municipality](/wiki/Direct-controlled_municipalities_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Direct-controlled municipalities of the People's Republic of China") would define it as prefecture-level, the district head of Pudong is given sub-provincial powers. In other words, it is *half a level* higher than what it would normally be.

## Ambiguity of the word "city" in China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_divisions_of_China&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Ambiguity of the word \"city\" in China")]

The Chinese word "市" (shì) is usually loosely translated into English as "city". However, it has several different meanings due to the complexity of the administrative divisions used in China. Despite being urban or having urban centers, the [SARs](/wiki/Special_administrative_regions_of_China "Special administrative regions of China") are almost never referred to as "Hong Kong City"/"Macau City" in contemporary Chinese and thus are not covered by the description below.

By its political level, when a "city" is referred to, it can be a:

* LV 1 (provincial-level):
  + [Municipality of China](/wiki/Municipalities_of_China "Municipalities of China"), literally "direct-controlled city" in Chinese, there being actually four: [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), [Tianjin](/wiki/Tianjin "Tianjin"), [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") and [Chongqing](/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing")
* LV 2 (prefecture-level):
  + [Sub-provincial city](/wiki/Sub-provincial_city "Sub-provincial city"), for example, [Shenzhen](/wiki/Shenzhen "Shenzhen") in [Guangdong Province](/wiki/Guangdong_Province "Guangdong Province")
  + [Prefecture-level city](/wiki/Prefecture-level_city "Prefecture-level city"), for example, [Shijiazhuang](/wiki/Shijiazhuang "Shijiazhuang"), capital of [Hebei Province](/wiki/Hebei_Province "Hebei Province")
* LV 3 (county-level):
  + [Sub-prefecture-level city](/wiki/Sub-prefecture-level_city "Sub-prefecture-level city"), for example, [Jiyuan](/wiki/Jiyuan "Jiyuan") (directly under the administration of [Henan Province](/wiki/Henan_Province "Henan Province"))
  + [County-level city](/wiki/County-level_city "County-level city"), for example, [Yiwu](/wiki/Yiwu "Yiwu") (under the administration of the prefecture-level city of [Jinhua](/wiki/Jinhua "Jinhua"))

By its actual area and population, it can be:

* Province-like, which is the municipality of [Chongqing](/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing"), a merger of 4 former prefectures and similar to the former Eastern-Sichuan province.
* Prefecture-like, which are the other three municipalities and almost all prefectural-level cities, usually 10–1,000 times larger than the urban center and a conglomeration of several counties and county-level cities. Some of them in sparsely populated areas like [Hulunbuir](/wiki/Hulunbuir "Hulunbuir") are even larger than Chongqing but have a population comparable to that of prefectures.
* County-like, which is all sub-prefecture-level and some county-level cities, and several extremely simple prefecture-level cities ([Jiayuguan](/wiki/Jiayuguan_City "Jiayuguan City"), [Xiamen](/wiki/Xiamen "Xiamen"), [Haikou](/wiki/Haikou "Haikou"), etc).
* Not substantially larger than urban establishment: some county-level cities, plus some members of the previous category. However, county-level cities converted from counties are unlikely to belong here. Shanghai, despite being prefecture-like in size, belongs here due to its subway already [extending beyond](/wiki/Line_11,_Shanghai_Metro "Line 11, Shanghai Metro") municipality limits. Some other economically prosperous prefecture-level cities are also provoking inter-prefecture urban integration, although they still possess (and never intend to eliminate) large swaths of rural area.

When used in the statistical data, the word "city" may have three different meanings:

* The area administrated by the city. For the municipality, the sub-provincial city, or the prefecture-level city, a "city" in this sense includes all of the counties, county-level cities, and [city districts](/wiki/District_(PRC)#Ethnic_districts "District (PRC)") that the city governs. For the [Sub-prefecture-level city](/wiki/Sub-prefecture-level_city "Sub-prefecture-level city") or the [County-level city](/wiki/County-level_city "County-level city"), it includes all of the [subdistricts](/wiki/Subdistricts_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Subdistricts of the People's Republic of China"), [towns](/wiki/Towns_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Towns of the People's Republic of China") and [townships](/wiki/Townships_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Townships of the People's Republic of China") that it has.
* The area comprising its urban [city districts](/wiki/District_(PRC)#Ethnic_districts "District (PRC)") and suburb [city districts](/wiki/Districts_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Districts of the People's Republic of China"). The difference between the urban district and the suburb districts is that an urban district comprises only the [subdistricts](/wiki/Subdistricts_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Subdistricts of the People's Republic of China"), while a suburb district also has [towns](/wiki/Towns_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Towns of the People's Republic of China") and [townships](/wiki/Townships_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Townships of the People's Republic of China") to govern rural areas. In some sense, this definition is approximately the [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area"). This definition is not applied to the sub-prefecture-level city and the county-level city since they do not have city districts under them.
  + Somewhat bizarrely, some districts such as [Haidian District](/wiki/Haidian_District "Haidian District") also possess towns. They have been treated clearly as urban districts for decades, but not from the inception, some areas are rural but other areas form an inseparable part of the central city.
* The urban area. Sometimes the urban area is referred as (Chinese: 市区; pinyin: *shìqū*). For the municipality, the sub-provincial city, and the prefecture-level city, it comprises the urban city district and the adjacent [subdistricts](/wiki/Subdistricts_of_China "Subdistricts of China") of the suburb city districts. For the sub-prefecture-level city and the county-level city, only central [subdistricts](/wiki/Subdistricts_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Subdistricts of the People's Republic of China") are included. This definition is close to the strict meaning of "city" in western countries.

The choice of definition of "city" used for statistical data of Chinese cities can lead to different results. For example, [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") is the largest city in China by population in the urban area but is smaller than [Chongqing](/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing") by the population within the administration area.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_divisions_of_China&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the administrative divisions of China](/wiki/History_of_the_administrative_divisions_of_China "History of the administrative divisions of China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Qing_Dynasty_1820.png/330px-Qing_Dynasty_1820.png)](/wiki/File:Qing_Dynasty_1820.png)

Qing China in 1820, with provinces in yellow, military governorates and protectorates in light yellow, tributary states in orange

Before the establishment of the [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty"), China was ruled by a network of kings, nobles, and tribes. The rivalry of these groups culminated in the [Warring States period](/wiki/Warring_States_period "Warring States period"), and the [state of Qin](/wiki/State_of_Qin "State of Qin") eventually emerged dominant.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/China_Administrative_Areas_-_DPLA_-_22a656e8833daa9fbe4263d11fb6d22f.jpg/330px-China_Administrative_Areas_-_DPLA_-_22a656e8833daa9fbe4263d11fb6d22f.jpg)](/wiki/File:China_Administrative_Areas_-_DPLA_-_22a656e8833daa9fbe4263d11fb6d22f.jpg)

Administrative units of China in 1948 ([CIA](/wiki/CIA "CIA") map)

The [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") was determined not to allow China to fall back into disunity, and therefore designed the first hierarchical administrative divisions in China, based on two levels: *jùn* [commanderies](/wiki/Commandery_(China) "Commandery (China)") and *xiàn* [counties](/wiki/Counties_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China#History "Counties of the People's Republic of China"). The [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") that came immediately after added *[zhōu](/wiki/Zhou_(political_division) "Zhou (political division)")* (usually translated as "provinces") as the third level on top, forming a three-tier structure.

The [Sui](/wiki/Sui_dynasty "Sui dynasty") and [Tang](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") dynasties abolished commanderies, and added [circuits](/wiki/Circuit_(political_division) "Circuit (political division)") (*dào*, later *lù* under the [Song](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") and [Jin](/wiki/Jin_dynasty_(1115%E2%80%931234) "Jin dynasty (1115–1234)")) on top, maintaining a three-tier system that lasted through the 13th century. (As a second-level division, *zhou* are translated as "prefectures".) The Mongol-established [Yuan dynasty](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty") introduced the modern precursors to [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_China "Provinces of China"), bringing the number of levels to four. This system was then kept more or less intact until the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty"), the last imperial dynasty to rule China.

The [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)") streamlined the levels to just provinces and counties in 1928 and made the first attempt to extend political administration beyond the county level by establishing [townships](/wiki/Townships_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Townships of the People's Republic of China") below counties. This was also the system officially adopted by the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") in 1949, which defined the administrative divisions of China as three levels: provinces, [counties](/wiki/County_(People%27s_Republic_of_China) "County (People's Republic of China)"), and [townships](/wiki/Townships_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Townships of the People's Republic of China").

In practice, however, more levels were inserted. The ROC government soon learned that it was not feasible for a province to directly govern tens and sometimes hundreds of counties. Started from Jiangxi province in 1935, [prefectures](/wiki/Prefecture_of_China "Prefecture of China") were later inserted between provinces and counties. They continued to be ubiquitously applied by the PRC government to nearly all areas of China until the 1980s. Since then, most of the prefectures were converted into prefecture-level cities. [Greater administrative areas](/wiki/Greater_administrative_area "Greater administrative area") were inserted on top of provinces by the PRC government, but they were soon abolished, in 1954. [District public offices](/wiki/District_public_office "District public office") were inserted between counties and townships; once ubiquitous as well, they are currently being abolished and very few remain.

The most recent major developments have been the establishment of [Chongqing](/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing") as a [municipality](/wiki/Municipality_of_China "Municipality of China") and the creation of [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") and [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau") as [special administrative regions](/wiki/Special_Administrative_Regions_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Special Administrative Regions of the People's Republic of China").

## Reform

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Administrative_divisions_of_China&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Reform")]

Main article: [Reform of the administrative divisions of China](/wiki/Reform_of_the_administrative_divisions_of_China "Reform of the administrative divisions of China")

In recent years there have been calls to reform the administrative divisions and levels of China. Rumours of an impending major reform have also spread through various online bulletin boards.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The [district public offices](/wiki/District_(PRC) "District (PRC)") is an ongoing reform to remove an extra level of administration from between the county and township levels. There have also been calls to abolish the prefecture-level, and some provinces have transferred some of the power prefectures currently hold to the counties they govern. There are also calls to reduce the size of the provinces. The ultimate goal is to reduce the different administration levels from five to three (Provincial level, County level, Village level), reducing the amount of [corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_China "Corruption in China") as well as the number of government workers, in order to lower the budget.
